pages.text: This section of the book contains a collection of beginner brews. These are simple brews that can be made with just a few ingredients, and are a great way to get started with brewing. They can also be used as a base for more complex brews. It's missing some information about the recipes, so you will need to experiment a bit.

pages.title: Absinthe
pages.text: What happens when you throw some toxic plants into a kettle and pray for the best? You get absinthe, a strong brew with a very distinct flavor. $(thing)Brew$() $(item)16 grass$() for a short time, and $(thing)distill$() it. It doesn't kill you, but it isn't healthy either.

pages.title: Beer
pages.text: One of the simplest brews to make, probably invented around 5000 BCE by the Sumerians. $(thing)Brew$() $(item)6 wheat$() for 8 minutes, and $(thing)age$() it for at least $(n)$(t:One in-game day is equal to 20 real minutes.)3 days$(). Aging it for longer can improve the taste, and its value.

pages.title: Dandelion Wine
pages.text: A nice, light wine, made from $(item)12 dandelions$() $(thing)brewed$() for a few minutes, $(thing)distilled$() with $(item)redstone$() and $(thing)aged$() for no more than $(n)$(t:One in-game day is equal to 20 real minutes.)10 days$(). Easy to get working, difficult to perfect.

pages.title: Rum
pages.text: Made in the Caribbean, a classic drink known for its luxury. $(thing)Brew$() $(item)14 sugarcane$() for a couple of minutes, $(thing)distill$() with $(item)glowstone dust$() and $(thing)age$() it in $(item)oak$() wood for $(n)$(t:One in-game day is equal to 20 real minutes.)more than 12 days$(). The longer you age it, the better it gets.

pages.title: Whiskey
pages.text: Originally a medicinal tonic crafted by British monks, it quickly evolved into a popular drink. $(thing)Brew$() $(item)some kelp$(), $(item)5 sugar$() and $(item)a little wheat$() for a short time, $(thing)distill$() and $(thing)age$() it in $(item)mangrove$() or $(item)cherry$() wood for at least $(n)$(t:One in-game day is equal to 20 real minutes.)7 days$(). The longer you age it, the stronger its flavor gets.
